findings.description: mask_middle with visible=0 (explicitly allowed since the guard is 'visible < 0', not '<= 0') reaches this line where value[-0:] evaluates as value[0:] and returns the ENTIRE secret in cleartext (e.g. mask_middle('supersecret', 0) -> '***********supersecret'), so this data-masking primitive leaks 100% of sensitive data — CWE-200 / OWASP A04.

levenshtein is O(len(a)*len(b)) with no input-size cap, so as a web-service entry point comparing attacker-controlled strings two ~100k-char inputs yield ~10^10 iterations and pin a CPU core per request — an unauthenticated algorithmic-complexity DoS (CWE-407 / OWASP A06).

mask_middle with mask_char='' drops the mask entirely — short values return '' (losing that data existed) and long values return first+last chars adjacent with no redaction indicator, weakening the masking-integrity guarantee (CWE-200-adjacent).

slugify uses NFKD normalization + ascii-ignore, so distinct Unicode inputs can normalize to identical ASCII slugs; if slugs are used downstream as keys/identifiers/filenames, collisions enable resource confusion or overwrite (CWE-1289).
slugify can silently return an empty string for all-invalid input; a caller that uses the slug in a path or as a key without an empty-string check inherits a sharp edge (e.g. empty path segment).
slugify with a caller/user-supplied negative max_length performs slug[:negative], silently truncating from the end instead of erroring or returning empty — surprising but not a data leak.

indent_block uses str.splitlines() (full Unicode line-boundary set: \r, \x0b, \x0c, \x85,  ,  , etc.) and re-joins with \n, silently altering line terminators; relevant only if the output is later fed into a line-oriented sink such as logs or HTTP headers.
